title: Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA)

theory.introduction: QAOA is a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm for combinatorial optimization. It alternates between a cost layer that rewards good answers and a mixer layer that keeps the state exploring the search space.
theory.why_it_matters: QAOA is one of the most important near-term optimization algorithms because it connects graph problems, variational circuits, and classical parameter tuning in one compact loop.
theory.core_idea: Prepare a broad starting state, apply a problem-dependent cost Hamiltonian with angle gamma, apply a mixer Hamiltonian with angle beta, then let a classical optimizer tune those angles to increase the expected objective value.
key_points: QAOA is hybrid: the quantum circuit evaluates a parameterized state while a classical optimizer updates the parameters.
The cost Hamiltonian encodes what makes an answer good.
The mixer Hamiltonian keeps amplitude moving between candidate answers.
A single layer is called p = 1. Larger p means more expressivity and usually more optimization difficulty.
For Max-Cut, good solutions place neighboring nodes in opposite partitions.

mathematical_representation.qaoa_state: |psi(beta, gamma)> = exp(-i beta B) exp(-i gamma C) |+...+>

sections.title: Optimization Lens
content: Every bitstring is a candidate solution.
The cost layer rewards good solutions with phase.
The mixer layer prevents the circuit from getting stuck on one answer too early.
The classical optimizer chooses the angles that make good answers more likely.

sections.title: Max-Cut Intuition
content: Each qubit labels which side of the cut a node belongs to.
An edge is satisfied when its endpoints land in different partitions.
Bitstrings like 01 and 10 cut a single edge, while 00 and 11 do not.
sections.intuition: QAOA does not search every answer separately. It reshapes one quantum state so good cuts collect more probability.

sections.title: Tradeoffs
content: Larger p can improve the best achievable answer.
More layers also mean more parameters and a harder optimization landscape.
Hardware noise and barren plateaus can make tuning difficult.

steps.description: Translate the optimization problem into a cost Hamiltonian.
Prepare the equal superposition state using Hadamards.
Apply the cost layer exp(-i gamma C).
Apply the mixer layer exp(-i beta B).
Measure the circuit and estimate the objective value.
Use a classical optimizer to choose better beta and gamma values.

circuit_examples.name: Single-Layer QAOA
circuit_examples.description: A p = 1 circuit starts in superposition, applies one cost layer, then one mixer layer.

circuit_examples.name: Two-Node Max-Cut
circuit_examples.description: For a graph with one edge, the desired answers are 01 and 10 because they place the nodes on opposite sides of the cut.

applications: Max-Cut and related graph partitioning problems
advantages: Fits naturally on near-term quantum hardware
Easy to connect to classical optimizers
Problem structure enters directly through the cost Hamiltonian
limitations: Deeper circuits face more hardware noise
Good performance often depends on problem-specific heuristics
summary: QAOA steers probability toward good answers by alternating cost and mixer layers. The quantum circuit proposes a distribution over bitstrings, and the classical optimizer keeps retuning the proposal.

simulation.section_subtitle: Tune beta and gamma on a tiny graph and watch probability flow toward the cut states.
try_this: Try small beta and gamma values first so you can see the distribution move gradually.
Compare the cut states 01 and 10 against the uncut states 00 and 11.
Look for parameter settings where the graph preview prefers opposite node colors.

quiz.question: QAOA is mainly designed for which class of tasks?
quiz.explanation: QAOA targets combinatorial optimization problems such as Max-Cut, routing, and scheduling.

quiz.question: What does the cost Hamiltonian represent?
options: A random mixer used only for exploration
The objective function that scores candidate answers
The number of qubits in the circuit
quiz.answer: The objective function that scores candidate answers
quiz.explanation: The cost Hamiltonian is the problem-dependent part of QAOA. It rewards good bitstrings through phase.

quiz.question: What is the role of the mixer layer?
options: It keeps amplitude moving through the search space
It resets the circuit after each layer
quiz.answer: It keeps amplitude moving through the search space
quiz.explanation: Without the mixer, the circuit would have no mechanism to explore alternative candidate bitstrings.

quiz.question: Why are 01 and 10 the best answers for a graph with one edge?
options: They put both nodes on the same side of the cut
They cut the edge by placing the nodes in opposite partitions
quiz.answer: They cut the edge by placing the nodes in opposite partitions
quiz.explanation: In Max-Cut, an edge contributes when its endpoints land on different sides. For a single edge, that means 01 and 10.
